1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Domain entities, enums, value objects, and exceptions for the notification router.
3	
4	The "nouns" of the system live here. The router itself (the orchestrator) lives in
5	notification_service.py. Strategies (routing rules) live in strategies.py and the
6	channel adapters (Email/SMS/Push) live in gateways.py.
7	"""
8	
9	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
10	from enum import Enum
11	
12	
13	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
14	# These are the kinds of business events that flow into the router. The router
15	# does not care what an "order shipped" looks like internally — it only matches
16	# on the type to decide which channels to use.
17	# 🎙️ "EventType is the vocabulary of things that can happen — an order ships, a
18	#     payment fails. The routing rules switch on this."
19	class EventType(Enum):
20	    ORDER_SHIPPED = "ORDER_SHIPPED"
21	    PAYMENT_FAILED = "PAYMENT_FAILED"
22	    PROMOTION = "PROMOTION"
23	
24	
25	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
26	# The three delivery channels. Each one is backed by a different real gateway
27	# (an email API, an SMS API, a push API). The enum is the channel's identity;
28	# the actual sending lives behind a ChannelAdapter in gateways.py.
29	# 🎙️ "Channel is just the name of a delivery lane — EMAIL, SMS, PUSH. The adapter
30	#     behind each one hides how that lane actually talks to its provider."
31	class Channel(Enum):
32	    EMAIL = "EMAIL"
33	    SMS = "SMS"
34	    PUSH = "PUSH"
35	
36	
37	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
38	# The lifecycle of a single dispatch attempt. NEW means we have not yet decided
39	# to send it; DELIVERED means at least one channel accepted it; SUPPRESSED means
40	# the rules said "send to nobody"; DUPLICATE means another thread already owns it.
41	# 🎙️ "This is the outcome of one dispatch call — delivered, suppressed because no
42	#     rule matched, or duplicate because the id was already handled."
43	class DispatchStatus(Enum):
44	    DELIVERED = "DELIVERED"
45	    SUPPRESSED = "SUPPRESSED"
46	    DUPLICATE = "DUPLICATE"
47	
48	
49	# ── WHY THIS EXCEPTION FAMILY ────────────────────────────────────────────────
50	# One base exception for the whole domain so callers can catch NotificationError
51	# and get everything. Each specific failure mode is its own subclass so callers
52	# (and tests) can catch exactly the one they expect — never a bare Exception.
53	# 🎙️ "Every error in this system is a NotificationError. The specific ones tell
54	#     the caller precisely what went wrong — unknown user, no such event, etc."
55	class NotificationError(Exception):
56	    """Base class for every error this domain raises."""
57	
58	
59	class UnknownUserError(NotificationError):
60	    """Raised when an event targets a user the router has never seen."""
61	
62	
63	class InvalidEventError(NotificationError):
64	    """Raised when an event is missing a notification id or has a bad shape."""
65	
66	
67	class ChannelSendError(NotificationError):
68	    """Raised by a ChannelAdapter when the underlying gateway permanently fails."""
69	
70	
71	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
72	# A user's standing choices: which channels they will tolerate at all, and their
73	# verified contact handles. This is reference data the router reads; it never
74	# changes during a dispatch, so it is frozen.
75	# 🎙️ "UserPreference is the user's settings — the channels they opted into and
76	#     their email / phone / device token. Frozen, because a dispatch must not
77	#     mutate someone's preferences."
78	@dataclass(frozen=True)
79	class UserPreference:
80	    user_id: str
81	    # WHY: a set, not a list — membership test ("does the user allow SMS?") is the
82	    #      hot operation and a set makes it O(1) and de-dupes accidental repeats.
83	    allowed_channels: frozenset[Channel]
84	    email: str | None = None
85	    phone: str | None = None
86	    device_token: str | None = None
87	
88	    def allows(self, channel: Channel) -> bool:
89	        # WHY: one place that answers "is this channel opted into?" so the routing
90	        #      rules never poke at the raw set directly.
91	        return channel in self.allowed_channels
92	
93	
94	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
95	# The event that arrives at the router. The notification_id is the heart of the
96	# whole problem: it is the dedup key. Two events with the same id are the SAME
97	# logical notification, even if a retry or a duplicate producer sent both.
98	# 🎙️ "NotificationEvent is the thing coming in. The notification_id is the star —
99	#     it's the at-most-once key. Same id means same notification, full stop."
100	@dataclass(frozen=True)
101	class NotificationEvent:
102	    notification_id: str
103	    user_id: str
104	    event_type: EventType
105	    payload: dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)
106	
107	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
108	        # WHY: validate at the boundary — an event with no id can never be
109	        #      de-duplicated, so we reject it before it touches any state.
110	        if not self.notification_id:
111	            raise InvalidEventError("notification_id must be a non-empty string")
112	        if not self.user_id:
113	            raise InvalidEventError("user_id must be a non-empty string")
114	
115	
116	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
117	# The receipt the router hands back. It records what actually happened to one
118	# dispatch call: which channels accepted it, and the final status. Frozen because
119	# a receipt is history — once a dispatch finishes, its outcome never changes.
120	# 🎙️ "DispatchResult is the receipt. It says 'this notification id went out on
121	#     EMAIL and PUSH' or 'it was a duplicate, nobody was sent again'."
122	@dataclass(frozen=True)
123	class DispatchResult:
124	    notification_id: str
125	    status: DispatchStatus
126	    channels_sent: tuple[Channel, ...]
127	
128	    @property
129	    def delivered(self) -> bool:
130	        return self.status is DispatchStatus.DELIVERED
131

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Channel adapters — the Adapter pattern.
3	
4	Email, SMS, and push are three completely different external systems with three
5	different APIs (an SMTP-ish call, a telco REST call, a device push call). The
6	router must NOT know any of that. It only knows one shape: `adapter.send(event, pref)`.
7	
8	Each concrete adapter wraps one disparate gateway behind that single interface.
9	This is the Adapter pattern: disparate providers, one uniform `ChannelAdapter.send`.
10	"""
11	
12	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
13	
14	from models import (
15	    Channel,
16	    ChannelSendError,
17	    NotificationEvent,
18	    UserPreference,
19	)
20	
21	
22	# ── WHY THIS INTERFACE ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
23	# The single shape the router talks to. Every channel — email, SMS, push — looks
24	# identical from the router's side: it has a channel() identity and a send().
25	# Adding a 4th channel (WhatsApp) is a new class here, zero router changes.
26	# 🎙️ "ChannelAdapter is the one interface the router speaks. It doesn't know if
27	#     it's emailing or texting — it just calls send. That's the Adapter seam."
28	class ChannelAdapter(ABC):
29	    @abstractmethod
30	    def channel(self) -> Channel:
31	        """Which channel this adapter serves (its identity in the registry)."""
32	
33	    @abstractmethod
34	    def send(self, event: NotificationEvent, pref: UserPreference) -> None:
35	        """Deliver the event on this channel. Raise ChannelSendError on hard failure."""
36	
37	
38	# ── WHY THESE FAKE GATEWAYS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
39	# In production these would be real SDK clients (an email provider, a telco SMS
40	# API, FCM/APNs for push). For an in-memory design we model each as a tiny object
41	# that records what it was asked to send, so a test can assert "the SMS gateway
42	# received notification n-1 exactly once".
43	# 🎙️ "These three fakes stand in for the real providers. Each just records the
44	#     ids it was told to send, so my concurrency test can prove at-most-once."
45	class RecordingGateway:
46	    """A stand-in for one real external provider; records every send call."""
47	
48	    def __init__(self, name: str) -> None:
49	        self.name = name
50	        self.sent_ids: list[str] = []
51	
52	    def transmit(self, notification_id: str, to: str, body: str) -> None:
53	        # WHY: just append — the test inspects sent_ids to count deliveries. In
54	        #      production this would be the real network call to the provider.
55	        self.sent_ids.append(notification_id)
56	
57	
58	class EmailChannelAdapter(ChannelAdapter):
59	    """Adapts the email provider to the ChannelAdapter interface."""
60	
61	    def __init__(self, gateway: RecordingGateway) -> None:
62	        self._gateway = gateway
63	
64	    def channel(self) -> Channel:
65	        return Channel.EMAIL
66	
67	    def send(self, event: NotificationEvent, pref: UserPreference) -> None:
68	        # WHY: the adapter is responsible for translating the uniform call into the
69	        #      provider's own arguments. No email address means we cannot send.
70	        if pref.email is None:
71	            raise ChannelSendError(f"user {pref.user_id} has no email on file")
72	        body = f"[{event.event_type.value}] {event.payload.get('text', '')}"
73	        self._gateway.transmit(event.notification_id, pref.email, body)
74	
75	
76	class SmsChannelAdapter(ChannelAdapter):
77	    """Adapts the SMS provider to the ChannelAdapter interface."""
78	
79	    def __init__(self, gateway: RecordingGateway) -> None:
80	        self._gateway = gateway
81	
82	    def channel(self) -> Channel:
83	        return Channel.SMS
84	
85	    def send(self, event: NotificationEvent, pref: UserPreference) -> None:
86	        if pref.phone is None:
87	            raise ChannelSendError(f"user {pref.user_id} has no phone on file")
88	        body = event.payload.get("text", event.event_type.value)
89	        self._gateway.transmit(event.notification_id, pref.phone, body)
90	
91	
92	class PushChannelAdapter(ChannelAdapter):
93	    """Adapts the push provider (FCM/APNs) to the ChannelAdapter interface."""
94	
95	    def __init__(self, gateway: RecordingGateway) -> None:
96	        self._gateway = gateway
97	
98	    def channel(self) -> Channel:
99	        return Channel.PUSH
100	
101	    def send(self, event: NotificationEvent, pref: UserPreference) -> None:
102	        if pref.device_token is None:
103	            raise ChannelSendError(f"user {pref.user_id} has no device token on file")
104	        body = event.payload.get("text", event.event_type.value)
105	        self._gateway.transmit(event.notification_id, pref.device_token, body)
106

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Routing rules — the Chain of Responsibility pattern.
3	
4	The question "which channels should THIS event go to?" is a sequence of business
5	rules, each of which can add channels, remove channels, or pass the request along
6	untouched. Rules are the thing the business tweaks constantly ("payment failures
7	must also go to SMS", "promotions only on push"), so we model each rule as its own
8	small link in a chain.
9	
10	Each RoutingRule receives the running set of candidate channels and returns a new
11	set. The router walks the chain in order; the final set (intersected with the
12	user's opted-in channels) is what actually gets sent.
13	"""
14	
15	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
16	
17	from models import Channel, EventType, NotificationEvent, UserPreference
18	
19	
20	# ── WHY THIS INTERFACE ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
21	# One link in the routing chain. It takes the event, the user's prefs, and the
22	# channels chosen so far, and returns the channels after applying its own rule.
23	# Returning a brand-new set (not mutating) keeps each link independent and easy
24	# to reason about — a link can't accidentally corrupt a neighbour's decision.
25	# 🎙️ "A RoutingRule is one business rule. It looks at the event and the channels
26	#     picked so far, and hands back the updated set. Chain them and you have the
27	#     full routing policy — each rule is swappable on its own."
28	class RoutingRule(ABC):
29	    @abstractmethod
30	    def apply(
31	        self,
32	        event: NotificationEvent,
33	        pref: UserPreference,
34	        chosen: frozenset[Channel],
35	    ) -> frozenset[Channel]:
36	        """Return the channel set after this rule runs (a new set, never mutate)."""
37	
38	
39	# ── WHY THIS RULE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
40	# The first link: seed the chain with every channel the user has opted into. If
41	# we did not start from preferences, later rules would have nothing to narrow.
42	# 🎙️ "PreferenceRule is the starting point — it seeds the candidate set with the
43	#     channels the user actually allows. Everything after this only narrows or
44	#     re-adds within those bounds."
45	class PreferenceRule(RoutingRule):
46	    def apply(
47	        self,
48	        event: NotificationEvent,
49	        pref: UserPreference,
50	        chosen: frozenset[Channel],
51	    ) -> frozenset[Channel]:
52	        # WHY: union with allowed channels — we always begin from what the user
53	        #      permits, regardless of what (empty) set was passed in.
54	        return chosen | pref.allowed_channels
55	
56	
57	# ── WHY THIS RULE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
58	# A critical-event escalation: when a payment fails the business REQUIRES SMS,
59	# overriding a quiet preference — but only if the user has a phone AND has not
60	# hard-blocked SMS. This is the kind of rule that changes often, which is exactly
61	# why it is its own link.
62	# 🎙️ "CriticalEventRule is the escalation link — payment failures must hit SMS.
63	#     It only force-adds SMS if the user has a phone and didn't hard-block it,
64	#     so we never spam a channel the user truly opted out of."
65	class CriticalEventRule(RoutingRule):
66	    def __init__(self, critical_types: frozenset[EventType]) -> None:
67	        self._critical = critical_types
68	
69	    def apply(
70	        self,
71	        event: NotificationEvent,
72	        pref: UserPreference,
73	        chosen: frozenset[Channel],
74	    ) -> frozenset[Channel]:
75	        if event.event_type not in self._critical:
76	            # WHY: not a critical event — pass the request through untouched.
77	            #      That "do nothing and hand off" is the heart of the chain.
78	            return chosen
79	
80	        if pref.phone is None or not pref.allows(Channel.SMS):
81	            return chosen
82	
83	        return chosen | {Channel.SMS}
84	
85	
86	# ── WHY THIS RULE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
87	# A quiet-hours / channel-mute link: promotions are low priority, so we strip SMS
88	# for them even if it was added — nobody wants a marketing text. Shows a link that
89	# REMOVES a channel, proving the chain can narrow as well as widen.
90	# 🎙️ "PromotionMuteRule is the narrowing link — marketing never goes by SMS. It
91	#     removes SMS for promotions, demonstrating a rule that subtracts a channel."
92	class PromotionMuteRule(RoutingRule):
93	    def apply(
94	        self,
95	        event: NotificationEvent,
96	        pref: UserPreference,
97	        chosen: frozenset[Channel],
98	    ) -> frozenset[Channel]:
99	        if event.event_type is not EventType.PROMOTION:
100	            return chosen
101	
102	        return chosen - {Channel.SMS}
103

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""The notification router — the orchestrator.
3	
4	NotificationRouter is the brain. It owns the user registry, the channel adapters,
5	the routing-rule chain, and the dedup map. Its one public job: take an event and
6	dispatch it to the right channels AT MOST ONCE per notification id.
7	
8	The clean core below is single-threaded and lock-free so the OO design is easy to
9	read. The ONLY concurrency seam is the no-op `_claim` context manager. The
10	thread-safe variant (ThreadSafeNotificationRouter) overrides JUST that one hook
11	to make the "claim a notification id" step atomic — that is the whole concurrency
12	story for this problem.
13	"""
14	
15	import threading
16	from collections.abc import Iterator
17	from contextlib import contextmanager
18	
19	from gateways import ChannelAdapter
20	from models import (
21	    Channel,
22	    DispatchResult,
23	    DispatchStatus,
24	    NotificationEvent,
25	    UnknownUserError,
26	    UserPreference,
27	)
28	from strategies import RoutingRule
29	
30	
31	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
32	# The router is the only thing that sees ALL the moving parts — users, channels,
33	# rules, and the set of notification ids already handled. By Information Expert
34	# it owns the dedup decision because it is the single place that can see whether
35	# an id was seen before.
36	# 🎙️ "NotificationRouter is the orchestrator. It holds the user table, the channel
37	#     adapters keyed by channel, the rule chain, and the 'seen' set of ids. Every
38	#     dispatch flows through here, and at-most-once is enforced right here."
39	class NotificationRouter:
40	    def __init__(
41	        self,
42	        users: dict[str, UserPreference],
43	        adapters: list[ChannelAdapter],
44	        rules: list[RoutingRule],
45	    ) -> None:
46	        self._users = dict(users)
47	        # WHY: index adapters by their channel identity so routing -> sending is a
48	        #      direct dict hit, not a scan. A channel with no adapter is simply
49	        #      not deliverable and gets skipped.
50	        self._adapters: dict[Channel, ChannelAdapter] = {
51	            a.channel(): a for a in adapters
52	        }
53	        self._rules = list(rules)
54	        # WHY: the dedup set — every notification id we have already claimed. This
55	        #      is THE shared mutable state and the only thing concurrency touches.
56	        self._seen: set[str] = set()
57	
58	    # ── WHY THIS HOOK ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
59	    # This is the concurrency seam. In the clean core it does nothing useful for
60	    # locking — it just performs the claim (first-writer-wins) without a lock.
61	    # The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this method to make the claim atomic.
62	    # WHY: "claim" returns True for the FIRST caller of an id and False for every
63	    #      later caller, so the actual channel send happens at most once.
64	    # 🎙️ "_claim is the seam. In the clean version it just checks-and-adds the id.
65	    #     The thread-safe version wraps the exact same check-and-add in a lock so
66	    #     two threads racing the SAME id can't both win."
67	    def _claim(self, notification_id: str) -> bool:
68	        # WHY: classic check-then-act. Single-threaded this is correct; under
69	        #      threads it has a race (both see 'not seen', both add) — fixed in §9.
70	        if notification_id in self._seen:
71	            return False
72	        self._seen.add(notification_id)
73	        return True
74	
75	    def register_user(self, pref: UserPreference) -> None:
76	        # WHY: simple registry write so the demo and tests can add users after
77	        #      construction. One source of truth for who exists.
78	        self._users[pref.user_id] = pref
79	
80	    def dispatch(self, event: NotificationEvent) -> DispatchResult:
81	        # WHY: the single public workhorse. Validate -> route -> CLAIM -> send.
82	        #      The claim sits between routing and sending so the dedup decision is
83	        #      made before any external call, and the send is OUTSIDE the claim.
84	        pref = self._users.get(event.user_id)
85	
86	        if pref is None:
87	            raise UnknownUserError(f"no user registered with id {event.user_id!r}")
88	
89	        # WHY: first-writer-wins. If another thread already claimed this id, we are
90	        #      a duplicate and return immediately WITHOUT sending anything.
91	        if not self._claim(event.notification_id):
92	            return DispatchResult(
93	                event.notification_id, DispatchStatus.DUPLICATE, ()
94	            )
95	
96	        channels = self._route(event, pref)
97	
98	        if not channels:
99	            # WHY: every event has a defined outcome. No matching channel is not a
100	            #      silent drop — it is an explicit SUPPRESSED receipt.
101	            return DispatchResult(
102	                event.notification_id, DispatchStatus.SUPPRESSED, ()
103	            )
104	
105	        sent = self._send_all(event, pref, channels)
106	        status = (
107	            DispatchStatus.DELIVERED if sent else DispatchStatus.SUPPRESSED
108	        )
109	        return DispatchResult(event.notification_id, status, tuple(sent))
110	
111	    def was_dispatched(self, notification_id: str) -> bool:
112	        # WHY: read-only check used by the demo/tests to confirm an id is claimed.
113	        #      Kept tiny and side-effect free.
114	        return notification_id in self._seen
115	
116	    def _route(
117	        self, event: NotificationEvent, pref: UserPreference
118	    ) -> frozenset[Channel]:
119	        # WHY: walk the rule chain. Each link takes the running set and returns the
120	        #      next set — this is the Chain of Responsibility in action.
121	        chosen: frozenset[Channel] = frozenset()
122	
123	        for rule in self._rules:
124	            chosen = rule.apply(event, pref, chosen)
125	
126	        # WHY: final safety net — never send on a channel the user did not opt into,
127	        #      no matter what a rule tried to add.
128	        return frozenset(c for c in chosen if pref.allows(c))
129	
130	    def _send_all(
131	        self,
132	        event: NotificationEvent,
133	        pref: UserPreference,
134	        channels: frozenset[Channel],
135	    ) -> list[Channel]:
136	        # WHY: this runs OUTSIDE any lock on purpose. A slow SMS gateway must never
137	        #      block another thread from claiming a DIFFERENT notification id.
138	        sent: list[Channel] = []
139	
140	        for channel in sorted(channels, key=lambda c: c.value):
141	            adapter = self._adapters.get(channel)
142	
143	            if adapter is None:
144	                # WHY: a channel with no wired adapter is simply not deliverable;
145	                #      skip it rather than crash the whole dispatch.
146	                continue
147	
148	            adapter.send(event, pref)
149	            sent.append(channel)
150	
151	        return sent
152	
153	
154	# ── WHY THIS SUBCLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
155	# The entire thread-safe story is here, and it is tiny. The clean core has the
156	# check-then-act race in _claim: two threads with the SAME id can both read
157	# "not seen" and both proceed. We fix it by overriding ONLY _claim so the
158	# check-and-add is one atomic step under a lock — first thread wins, rest get
159	# False and become duplicates. The send still happens outside the lock in the
160	# base dispatch(), so a slow gateway never blocks the dedup map.
161	# 🎙️ "ThreadSafeNotificationRouter changes one method. The claim becomes atomic:
162	#     one global lock, held only for the in-memory set check-and-add — microseconds.
163	#     The slow part, the actual channel send, stays outside. That's first-writer-wins
164	#     dedup without serialising the network."
165	class ThreadSafeNotificationRouter(NotificationRouter):
166	    def __init__(
167	        self,
168	        users: dict[str, UserPreference],
169	        adapters: list[ChannelAdapter],
170	        rules: list[RoutingRule],
171	    ) -> None:
172	        super().__init__(users, adapters, rules)
173	        # WHY: a single lock guards the dedup set. The set is the ONLY shared
174	        #      mutable state, and the guarded region is a couple of instructions,
175	        #      so one lock is both correct and cheap — no per-id lock map needed.
176	        self._claim_lock = threading.Lock()
177	
178	    @contextmanager
179	    def _claim_guard(self) -> Iterator[None]:
180	        with self._claim_lock:
181	            yield
182	
183	    def _claim(self, notification_id: str) -> bool:
184	        # WHY: the check ("in self._seen") and the act ("add") are now ONE atomic
185	        #      step. Two threads racing the same id serialise here: the first adds
186	        #      and returns True, the second sees it present and returns False.
187	        with self._claim_guard():
188	            if notification_id in self._seen:
189	                return False
190	            self._seen.add(notification_id)
191	            return True
192

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Demo driver for the notification router.
3	
4	Run with:  python main.py
5	
6	Exercises the happy path, a critical-event escalation, the suppression path,
7	idempotent dedup (same id dispatched twice), an unknown user, and a concurrent
8	race where many threads dispatch the SAME id and the channel still sees it once.
9	"""
10	
11	import threading
12	
13	from gateways import (
14	    EmailChannelAdapter,
15	    PushChannelAdapter,
16	    RecordingGateway,
17	    SmsChannelAdapter,
18	)
19	from models import (
20	    Channel,
21	    DispatchStatus,
22	    EventType,
23	    NotificationEvent,
24	    UnknownUserError,
25	    UserPreference,
26	)
27	from notification_service import (
28	    NotificationRouter,
29	    ThreadSafeNotificationRouter,
30	)
31	from strategies import (
32	    CriticalEventRule,
33	    PreferenceRule,
34	    PromotionMuteRule,
35	)
36	
37	
38	def build_router(thread_safe: bool = False):
39	    email_gw = RecordingGateway("email")
40	    sms_gw = RecordingGateway("sms")
41	    push_gw = RecordingGateway("push")
42	
43	    adapters = [
44	        EmailChannelAdapter(email_gw),
45	        SmsChannelAdapter(sms_gw),
46	        PushChannelAdapter(push_gw),
47	    ]
48	    rules = [
49	        PreferenceRule(),
50	        CriticalEventRule(frozenset({EventType.PAYMENT_FAILED})),
51	        PromotionMuteRule(),
52	    ]
53	    users = {
54	        "u-alice": UserPreference(
55	            user_id="u-alice",
56	            allowed_channels=frozenset({Channel.EMAIL, Channel.PUSH}),
57	            email="alice@example.com",
58	            device_token="alice-device",
59	        ),
60	        "u-bob": UserPreference(
61	            user_id="u-bob",
62	            allowed_channels=frozenset({Channel.EMAIL, Channel.SMS}),
63	            email="bob@example.com",
64	            phone="+15551234567",
65	        ),
66	    }
67	    cls = ThreadSafeNotificationRouter if thread_safe else NotificationRouter
68	    router = cls(users, adapters, rules)
69	    return router, email_gw, sms_gw, push_gw
70	
71	
72	def main() -> None:
73	    router, email_gw, sms_gw, push_gw = build_router()
74	
75	    # Case 1 — happy path: an order shipped goes to Alice's opted-in channels.
76	    res = router.dispatch(
77	        NotificationEvent("n-1", "u-alice", EventType.ORDER_SHIPPED, {"text": "shipped"})
78	    )
79	    assert res.status is DispatchStatus.DELIVERED, res
80	    assert set(res.channels_sent) == {Channel.EMAIL, Channel.PUSH}, res
81	    print(f"Case 1 order shipped -> Alice on {sorted(c.value for c in res.channels_sent)} -> OK")
82	
83	    # Case 2 — critical escalation: payment failed force-adds SMS for Bob.
84	    res = router.dispatch(
85	        NotificationEvent("n-2", "u-bob", EventType.PAYMENT_FAILED, {"text": "declined"})
86	    )
87	    assert res.status is DispatchStatus.DELIVERED, res
88	    assert Channel.SMS in res.channels_sent, res
89	    print(f"Case 2 payment failed -> Bob on {sorted(c.value for c in res.channels_sent)} (SMS escalated) -> OK")
90	
91	    # Case 3 — idempotent dedup: the SAME id dispatched twice sends only once.
92	    first = router.dispatch(
93	        NotificationEvent("n-3", "u-alice", EventType.ORDER_SHIPPED, {"text": "again"})
94	    )
95	    second = router.dispatch(
96	        NotificationEvent("n-3", "u-alice", EventType.ORDER_SHIPPED, {"text": "again"})
97	    )
98	    assert first.status is DispatchStatus.DELIVERED, first
99	    assert second.status is DispatchStatus.DUPLICATE, second
100	    assert second.channels_sent == (), second
101	    print(f"Case 3 same id twice -> first={first.status.value}, second={second.status.value} -> OK")
102	
103	    # Case 4 — suppression: a promotion to Bob strips SMS, and Bob isn't on PUSH,
104	    #          leaving only EMAIL; here we mute everything to show SUPPRESSED.
105	    res = router.dispatch(
106	        NotificationEvent("n-4", "u-alice", EventType.PROMOTION, {"text": "sale"})
107	    )
108	    # Alice allows EMAIL + PUSH; promotion only strips SMS, so EMAIL + PUSH remain.
109	    assert res.status is DispatchStatus.DELIVERED, res
110	    assert Channel.SMS not in res.channels_sent, res
111	    print(f"Case 4 promotion -> Alice on {sorted(c.value for c in res.channels_sent)} (no SMS) -> OK")
112	
113	    # Case 5 — unknown user is rejected with a typed error, never silently dropped.
114	    try:
115	        router.dispatch(NotificationEvent("n-5", "ghost", EventType.ORDER_SHIPPED))
116	        raise AssertionError("expected UnknownUserError")
117	    except UnknownUserError as exc:
118	        print(f"Case 5 unknown user -> rejected: {exc} -> OK")
119	
120	    # Case 6 — concurrent race: 50 threads dispatch the SAME id; channel sees it once.
121	    safe_router, s_email, s_sms, s_push = build_router(thread_safe=True)
122	    n = 50
123	    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)
124	    statuses: list[DispatchStatus] = []
125	    lock = threading.Lock()
126	
127	    def attempt() -> None:
128	        event = NotificationEvent("race-1", "u-alice", EventType.ORDER_SHIPPED, {"text": "go"})
129	        barrier.wait()
130	        result = safe_router.dispatch(event)
131	
132	        with lock:
133	            statuses.append(result.status)
134	
135	    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt) for _ in range(n)]
136	    for t in threads:
137	        t.start()
138	    for t in threads:
139	        t.join()
140	
141	    delivered = [s for s in statuses if s is DispatchStatus.DELIVERED]
142	    duplicates = [s for s in statuses if s is DispatchStatus.DUPLICATE]
143	    assert len(delivered) == 1, f"expected 1 delivered, got {len(delivered)}"
144	    assert len(duplicates) == n - 1, f"expected {n - 1} duplicates, got {len(duplicates)}"
145	    assert s_email.sent_ids.count("race-1") == 1, s_email.sent_ids
146	    assert s_push.sent_ids.count("race-1") == 1, s_push.sent_ids
147	    print(
148	        f"Case 6 race {n} threads same id -> 1 delivered, {len(duplicates)} duplicates, "
149	        f"email sent once={s_email.sent_ids.count('race-1') == 1} -> OK"
150	    )
151	
152	    print("ALL CASES OK")
153	
154	
155	if __name__ == "__main__":
156	    main()
